title: What Is a Game? The Core Loop
html:
Defining the thing we design

Before you can design a game well, you need a working definition of what a game actually is. The most useful one for designers is short: a game is a series of interesting decisions (Sid Meier). Notice what that excludes. A decision that is obvious is not interesting. A decision with no consequence is not interesting. A decision you cannot understand is not a decision at all, just a guess. Your whole job is to keep producing choices that the player can grasp, that genuinely matter, and whose best answer is not obvious.

A broader academic definition adds structure: a game is a rule-based system with a variable, quantifiable outcome, where outcomes are assigned value, the player exerts effort to influence the outcome, feels attached to it, and the real-life consequences are negotiable (Juul). Strip out any one of those and the experience drifts toward something else: a toy (no goal), a puzzle (one correct outcome), or gambling (consequences not negotiable).

The core loop

Every game runs on a core loop: the smallest cycle of actions the player repeats over and over, moment to moment. In a shooter it might be spot enemy, aim, shoot, reposition. In a builder it might be gather, craft, place, expand. The core loop is where most of your players' time is spent, so it must feel good in isolation, before any story, art, or progression is added. If the loop is not fun after thirty seconds of greyboxes, no amount of content will save it.

Loops nest. The moment-to-moment loop (seconds) sits inside a session loop (minutes: clear a room, win a match) which sits inside a progression loop (hours: unlock, level up, master). Each loop should hand off cleanly to the next, giving the player a reason to start the cycle again the instant it ends.

anatomy of a core loop
The four beats that make a loop feel complete: 1. ACTION — the player does something (move, shoot, place, choose) 2. CHALLENGE — the system resists or complicates it 3. FEEDBACK — the game shows the result clearly and quickly 4. REWARD — progress, resources, or knowledge that fuels the next action Break any beat and the loop stalls: no challenge = boring; no feedback = confusing; no reward = pointless.

Interesting decisions in practice

A decision becomes interesting when options are comparable but distinct and each carries a tradeoff. Should I spend my last health potion now or save it? Take the safe long route or the risky shortcut? Buy armor or a better weapon? The classic test: if you can name one option as strictly best in every situation, it is not a choice, it is a chore. Designers call a useless never-correct option a trap and an always-correct one a dominant strategy; both kill decisions.

Tip.
When a mechanic feels flat, ask: what is the interesting decision here? If you cannot name one, you have a toy, not a game. Add a cost, a risk, or a scarce resource until a real tradeoff appears.

Verbs over nouns

Players remember verbs: what they got to do. Mario is about jumping; Portal is about teleporting; Tetris is about fitting. Strong games usually have one or two signature verbs and explore every consequence of them. When pitching a design, finish the sentence "the player gets to ___" with a verb, not a setting or a story. The setting decorates the verb; the verb is the game.

🎮 Design This: Name the Loop

Goal: practice seeing the core loop inside games you already know.

  1. Pick three games you have played a lot.
  2. For each, write its moment-to-moment loop as 3-4 verbs in a cycle.
  3. Identify the single interesting decision repeated most often in that loop.
  4. Name the signature verb in one word.
  5. Find one game where you cannot name a real decision, and explain why it feels like a chore.

title: The MDA Framework
html:
Three lenses on one game

MDA is the most widely taught formal framework in game design, introduced by Hunicke, LeBlanc, and Zubek. It splits a game into three connected layers: Mechanics, Dynamics, and Aesthetics. Its central insight is that designers and players approach a game from opposite ends. You build mechanics; the player experiences aesthetics. Understanding that gap is what stops you from designing for yourself instead of your audience.

the MDA chain
DESIGNER builds left-to-right → PLAYER feels right-to-left MECHANICS — the rules and components you author directly DYNAMICS — the run-time behavior that emerges when players engage the mechanics AESTHETICS — the emotional responses evoked in the player You can only TUNE mechanics. Dynamics and aesthetics you can only INFLUENCE.

Mechanics

Mechanics are the concrete rules, actions, and data: how movement works, what a card does, the damage formula, the economy of a resource. These are the only things you literally write. They are precise and controllable. A mechanic in chess is "the bishop moves diagonally any number of unblocked squares." Nothing emotional yet, just a rule.

Dynamics

Dynamics are the behaviors that arise when those rules meet real players over time. No one programmed "bluffing" into poker; it emerges from hidden cards plus betting. No one designed "camping" into a shooter; it emerges from sightlines plus respawn rules. Dynamics are where games surprise their own creators. You cannot author a dynamic directly. You set up mechanics and watch what the system does, then adjust the mechanics to encourage good dynamics and discourage toxic ones.

Aesthetics

Aesthetics here does not mean visual style. It means the kinds of fun the player feels. The MDA paper lists eight, a far more useful vocabulary than the word "fun":

  • Sensation — game as sense-pleasure (juicy feedback, beauty)
  • Fantasy — game as make-believe (be a pilot, a wizard)
  • Narrative — game as unfolding story
  • Challenge — game as obstacle course (mastery, difficulty)
  • Fellowship — game as social framework (co-op, community)
  • Discovery — game as uncharted territory (exploration)
  • Expression — game as self-discovery (building, customizing)
  • Submission — game as pastime (relaxing routine, idle play)

Designing backward from aesthetics

The power move is to start at the player's end. Decide which aesthetics you want to evoke, infer the dynamics that produce them, then engineer mechanics that generate those dynamics. Want tension (a flavor of Challenge)? You need a dynamic of mounting pressure, which you might produce with a shrinking timer, scarce ammo, and a save system that punishes mistakes. Want fellowship? You need interdependence, which you produce with mechanics no single player can solve alone.

Law.
You tune mechanics, but players only feel aesthetics. Never argue with a playtester about how the game "should" feel. Their aesthetic response is the ground truth; your job is to change the mechanics until the response matches your intent.

A worked example

Take a deck-builder. Aesthetic target: Expression plus Discovery. Desired dynamic: players assemble surprising combos that feel personal. Required mechanics: a large card pool, synergy keywords, randomized offers each run, and a deck-thinning option so combos become reliable. If players instead converge on one identical optimal deck, your Expression aesthetic has failed, and the fix lives in the mechanics layer (rebalance the dominant cards, widen synergies), not in lecturing players to "be more creative."

🎮 Design This: MDA Teardown

Goal: trace one game through all three layers and one emergent dynamic.

  1. Pick a multiplayer or systemic game you know well.
  2. List 4 core mechanics (precise rules).
  3. Name 2 dynamics that emerged but were never explicitly designed.
  4. Tag each dynamic with the primary aesthetic (from the 8) it serves.
  5. Pick one dynamic you dislike and propose the single mechanic change that would discourage it.

title: Game Feel and Juice
html:
The feel of control

Game feel is the moment-to-moment tactile sensation of controlling something in a virtual space. Steve Swink defines it through three pillars: real-time control (your input maps to motion instantly), simulated space (objects with mass, friction, momentum), and polish (the effects that amplify and sell every interaction). Two games with identical rules can feel like a luxury car and a shopping cart depending entirely on feel. This is craft, and it is mostly invisible until it is missing.

Juice: maximum output for minimum input

Juice is the layer of non-essential feedback that makes a game feel alive and responsive. The mantra: a juicy game returns a large, satisfying response to every small player action. Press a button and the world should react with sound, motion, color, and aftermath far out of proportion to the keystroke. Crucially, juice changes nothing about the rules; a juiced game and a dry game can be mechanically identical. Juice is pure presentation, and it is the difference between a prototype and a product.

the juice toolbox
Stack several of these on a single action; juice compounds. SQUASH & STRETCH — deform on impact/jump; sells weight and elasticity SCREENSHAKE — short, sharp camera jolt on big hits (use sparingly) PARTICLES — sparks, dust, debris that confirm contact HIT-STOP — freeze a few frames on impact; makes hits feel heavy FLASH/COLOR — brief white/tint flash on a struck target SOUND — punchy, layered SFX timed to the frame of contact EASING — never start/stop instantly; accelerate and decelerate ANTICIPATION — a wind-up before a big action telegraphs and rewards it

Easing, anticipation, follow-through

Three animation principles do the heavy lifting. Easing means motion accelerates in and decelerates out rather than snapping; linear motion reads as robotic and cheap. Anticipation is a brief opposite-direction wind-up before an action (the crouch before a jump, the pull-back before a punch); it primes the eye and makes the payoff land harder. Follow-through is the overshoot-and-settle after motion stops; a menu that slides in, overshoots a few pixels, then settles feels handmade. These come straight from classic animation and translate directly to interface and gameplay alike.

Hit-stop and screenshake, the heavy hitters

Hit-stop (a few frames of frozen time at the instant of impact) is perhaps the single highest-impact juice technique for combat. It gives the brain a microsecond to register the collision and makes weak hits feel like real blows. Screenshake is intoxicating but dangerous: a little sells power, too much induces nausea and hides the action. Keep it short, decay it fast, and let players reduce it. The famous Vlambeer talk "The Art of Screenshake" shows a plain shooter transformed into a thrilling one purely by stacking juice, no rule changes at all.

Danger.
Juice can mask a broken core loop in a demo and fool you (and investors). Always greybox the loop with zero juice first. If it is not at least mildly engaging dry, juice is lipstick on a corpse. Prove the loop, then juice it.

Feedback budgets and clarity

Juice competes with clarity. Every particle, shake, and flash is visual noise that can bury information the player needs. Reserve your loudest feedback for your most important events. If a minor pickup and a boss death both explode the screen, neither reads as special. Think of feedback as a budget: spend big on rare, meaningful moments and stay quiet on routine ones. Accessibility matters too: offer toggles for screenshake, flashing, and motion so the experience does not exclude players prone to motion sickness or photosensitivity.

Tip.
Time your sound to the exact frame of visual impact. Audio leading or lagging the visual by even 50ms makes a hit feel mushy. Sync is most of what makes feedback feel "crunchy."
🎮 Design This: Juice Spec

Goal: design the full feedback stack for a single action without writing engine code.

  1. Pick one verb: a melee hit, a jump-land, or collecting a coin.
  2. List every juice layer you would attach (from the toolbox), in trigger order.
  3. Sketch a timeline in frames: anticipation, impact, hit-stop, follow-through.
  4. Decide what is loud (rare, important) vs quiet (routine) to protect clarity.
  5. Note one accessibility toggle you would ship for this action.

title: Level Design and Teaching Without Text
html:
Levels as composed experiences

Level design is the craft of arranging space, challenge, and information so the player has a deliberately shaped experience. A level is not a container for content; it is an argument the designer makes about how the mechanics should be enjoyed, in what order, at what intensity. Great level design feels like the player is making free choices while quietly being guided down a path the designer authored.

Teach mechanics without text

The gold standard is teaching through play, not tutorial pop-ups. The classic case study is World 1-1 of Super Mario Bros., which teaches the entire game in its opening seconds without a single word. The player spawns on the left facing right (so the only direction is forward). The first enemy walks into them, teaching that touching enemies is bad. A jump over it teaches the jump button. A floating block with a coin teaches that jumping into blocks rewards you. Each idea is introduced in a safe space where failure costs little, then escalated where it counts.

the teach-test-twist pattern
Introduce one mechanic at a time using this three-beat rhythm: TEACH — present the mechanic in a SAFE, controlled space (no death) TEST — require the player to use it under mild pressure TWIST — combine it with a known mechanic, or subvert expectations Then leave it alone until you are ready to combine it with the NEXT idea. Nintendo internally calls a version of this kishotenketsu: intro, develop, twist, conclude.

Guiding the eye: sightlines and affordances

Players go where you point them, usually without noticing. Sightlines are deliberate framing that draws the eye to the next objective: a shaft of light, a distinctive landmark on the horizon, a path of brighter color, leading lines in the architecture. Affordances are visual cues that signal interactivity, often through a consistent visual language: yellow paint or ledges of a certain color mean "climbable," a particular gleam means "interactable." Once you establish a language, hold to it ruthlessly; one inconsistent cue erodes trust in all of them.

Designers also use negative space and contrast to control attention. The eye is drawn to the unique element in a uniform field, to motion in stillness, to warm color in a cool scene, to light in dark. You can pull a player across a huge open level using nothing but a single bright, moving, contrasting beacon.

Pacing: the intensity curve

A good level breathes. Relentless intensity exhausts; relentless calm bores. Chart your level as an intensity curve with peaks (combat, danger, spectacle) and valleys (exploration, puzzle, narrative, safe-rooms). Valleys let players process, restock, and feel relief, which makes the next peak hit harder. The overall trend usually rises toward a climax, but with a sawtooth of rest between spikes. The valley right before a boss is one of the most important beats in the whole level.

Law.
Introduce a mechanic in safety, then demand it in danger. Never debut a new idea in the exact moment failure is most punishing; the player cannot tell a fair challenge from a cheap trick they were never taught.

The four keys: kinds of fun in space

Different players seek different fun, and levels can cater to several at once. A common shorthand (Lazzaro's four keys): hard fun (challenge and mastery), easy fun (curiosity and exploration), serious fun (relaxation, meaning, rhythm), and people fun (social interaction). A strong level offers a critical path for the challenge-seeker while hiding optional rooms and secrets for the explorer, so multiple player types each get their kind of fun from the same space.

Tip.
Block out the level in plain grey shapes first (a greybox). If it is not fun as featureless boxes, art will not save it. The fun lives in the layout, spacing, and pacing, not the textures.
🎮 Design This: Greybox a Tutorial Level

Goal: design, on paper, a wordless opening level that teaches one mechanic.

  1. Pick one mechanic (double-jump, dash-through-gaps, push-blocks).
  2. Sketch a top-down or side-on greybox using only labeled boxes and arrows.
  3. Mark the TEACH (safe), TEST (pressure), and TWIST (combine) zones.
  4. Draw one sightline or affordance that points the player forward without text.
  5. Plot the intensity curve underneath as a simple peaks-and-valleys line.

title: Difficulty, Balancing, and Accessibility
html:
Difficulty is a curve, not a number

Difficulty is not one setting; it is a curve traced across the whole game. Players arrive knowing nothing and leave (ideally) as experts, and the challenge must rise to track their growing skill. Plot the challenge curve against the skill curve. If challenge outpaces skill, the player feels anxiety and quits. If skill outpaces challenge, the player feels boredom and drifts. The sweet spot between them, where the two curves run close together, is the flow channel, the zone of total absorption.

flow channel
Challenge must rise in step with player skill. ANXIETY — challenge >> skill (too hard, player rage-quits) FLOW — challenge ≈ skill (absorbed, time disappears) BOREDOM — challenge << skill (too easy, player wanders off) Real curves zig-zag: spike to a peak, drop to recover, spike higher. Sawtooth, not a ramp.

Fairness: the unwritten contract

Hard is fine; unfair is not. Fairness is a contract: the game promises that with enough skill and knowledge, every challenge is beatable, and that deaths are the player's fault, not the game's. Key fairness rules: telegraph attacks so a skilled player can react; never kill the player with information they could not have had (no off-screen instant-death they could not anticipate the first time); make failure legible so the player learns why they lost; and keep inputs responsive so a death is never blamed on the controls. The feeling "that was BS" is a design bug, not a player flaw.

Balancing tools

Balancing is tuning the relationships between options so the game stays interesting. Watch for two failure modes from Lesson 1: a dominant strategy (one option is always best, collapsing all decisions into it) and a trap option (one option is never worth taking). The goal is usually asymmetric balance: options that are very different yet roughly equal in value, like rock-paper-scissors where no single throw dominates. Useful techniques include soft counters (every strong option has a weakness), cost scaling (power must be paid for in time, risk, or resources), and diminishing returns (stacking the same thing yields less each time, discouraging one-note builds).

Difficulty options and accessibility

Difficulty settings are not a failure of design; they are respect for a wide audience. Beyond easy/normal/hard, consider granular toggles: separate sliders for combat lethality, puzzle hints, timer pressure, and platforming precision, so players can soften the part that frustrates them without trivializing the part they love. Accessibility overlaps heavily here: colorblind-safe palettes, remappable controls, subtitles and visual sound cues, reduced-motion and screenshake toggles, hold-vs-tap options, and aim or navigation assists. These features expand who can play your game at near-zero cost to players who do not need them, and they frequently improve the game for everyone.

Tip.
Treat accessibility as default, not an afterthought. Remappable controls, subtitles, and a colorblind check should be in the first prototype, not bolted on before launch. Retrofitting is far more expensive than designing for it from the start.

Dynamic difficulty adjustment

Dynamic difficulty adjustment (DDA) quietly tunes challenge to the individual player in real time: easing enemy aggression after repeated deaths, or nudging it up when the player breezes through. Done invisibly, it keeps more players in the flow channel. Done clumsily, it feels patronizing or is exploited (players who notice the "rubber band" may lose on purpose to get easier fights). The famous example of generous-but-hidden help is the way many shooters silently widen hit detection or grant a frame of extra survival at one health, never announced, always felt as "that was close."

Danger.
Visible rubber-banding punishes skilled play and undermines the fairness contract. If players can feel the game helping or hurting them based on their performance, they stop trusting that their wins are earned. Keep DDA subtle or make it an explicit, opt-in setting.
🎮 Design This: Tune a Difficulty Curve

Goal: design the difficulty progression for a 5-encounter level on paper.

  1. List five encounters and rate each 1-10 for intended challenge.
  2. Plot them as a curve; ensure a recovery valley before the hardest spike.
  3. For one encounter, write the telegraph that keeps it fair.
  4. Spot a possible dominant strategy and propose a soft counter for it.
  5. List three accessibility toggles you would ship with this level.

title: Systems Design, Economies, and Emergence
html:
From mechanics to systems

A single mechanic is a rule. A system is a set of mechanics that interact through resources and feedback to produce ongoing behavior. Systems design is the art of arranging those interactions so the whole becomes richer than the sum of its parts. Where a level is authored content (it plays out roughly the same each time), a system is a generator: it produces fresh situations every session. The best systemic games get more interesting the longer you study them because the parts keep recombining.

Economies: sources, sinks, and flow

Almost every game has an economy: resources that are created, stored, converted, and destroyed. Model it with three terms. A source creates resource (killing enemies drops gold). A sink removes it (buying gear, repair costs, upkeep). A converter transforms one resource into another (smelting ore into ingots). Balance is fundamentally about matching the rate of sources to the rate of sinks. Too many sources with weak sinks and the economy inflates: money becomes meaningless, choices evaporate. Too many sinks and players feel starved and grindy.

economy flow
Every resource needs a faucet and a drain. Track both. SOURCE (faucet) → resource enters the system (loot, harvest, income) STORAGE → resource is held (wallet, inventory cap) CONVERTER → one resource becomes another (craft, smelt, trade) SINK (drain) → resource leaves the system (purchase, upkeep, decay) Rule of thumb: design the SINKS first. Most economies break from too few drains, not too few faucets.

Feedback loops

Systems are governed by feedback loops, and recognizing them is the core skill of a systems designer. A positive (reinforcing) feedback loop amplifies a lead: winning gives you resources that help you win more. It creates snowball and runaway states, which can feel powerful but often end games early and make comebacks impossible. A negative (balancing) feedback loop resists a lead and pulls the system back toward equilibrium: the classic blue-shell catch-up mechanic, or rubber-banding in racers. Negative loops keep games close and tense but can feel unfair if they punish skill too bluntly.

Most good designs blend the two. Early game leans on positive loops so player choices feel impactful and progress feels real; late game introduces negative loops so matches stay competitive and reach a decisive end rather than dragging. Knowing which loop dominates which phase is how you control pacing at the systemic level.

Law.
Positive feedback ends games; negative feedback prolongs them. Use positive loops to make progress feel meaningful, and negative loops to keep the outcome uncertain. A game with only positive loops snowballs into a foregone conclusion.

Emergence

Emergence is the holy grail of systems design: complex, surprising, meaningful behavior arising from a few simple, well-chosen rules. Conway's Game of Life produces gliders and oscillators from four rules. Minecraft produces redstone computers from block-placement and a logic signal. Chess produces millions of strategies from six piece types. Emergence comes from rules that interact, not rules that pile up. The designer's leverage is to choose a small set of orthogonal mechanics whose combinations multiply, rather than authoring every situation by hand.

Contrast emergence with scripted or authored content. Scripted moments are reliable and can be tuned for maximum drama, but they are finite and identical on replay. Emergent systems are inexhaustible and personal but harder to control and balance. Strong games use both: a systemic sandbox for replayable depth, with scripted set-pieces layered on top for guaranteed high points.

Tip.
Prefer orthogonal mechanics that each do something different and combine cleanly. Fire that spreads, wood that burns, and wind that pushes fire generate emergent stories. Three mechanics that all just deal damage do not; they are the same idea wearing three hats.
🎮 Design This: Sketch an Economy

Goal: design a small resource economy on paper and stress-test its loops.

  1. Pick a game premise (survival camp, shop sim, tower defense).
  2. List every resource, then label each source, sink, and converter.
  3. Draw arrows showing flow; circle any resource with no sink (a leak).
  4. Identify one positive and one negative feedback loop in your design.
  5. Predict the dominant strategy and add one sink that disrupts it.

title: Player Psychology and Motivation
html:
Why people keep playing

Mechanics give players things to do; psychology explains why they keep doing them. A designer who understands motivation can build engagement honestly, and can also recognize when a system has tipped into manipulation. The two foundational ideas are flow and self-determination theory.

Flow

Flow, from psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, is the state of complete absorption where time vanishes and action feels effortless. Games are arguably the most reliable flow-delivery machines ever built. Flow requires: a challenge matched to skill (Lesson 5), clear goals so the player always knows what to do next, and immediate feedback so they always know how they are doing. Remove any one and flow collapses: unclear goals breed aimlessness, missing feedback breeds doubt, mismatched challenge breeds anxiety or boredom.

conditions for flow
All three must hold at once, moment to moment: 1. CHALLENGE ≈ SKILL — hard enough to engage, not to overwhelm 2. CLEAR GOALS — the player always knows what to do next 3. IMMEDIATE FEEDBACK — the player always knows how they are doing Result: merged action and awareness, lost sense of time, intrinsic enjoyment.

Intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation

Self-determination theory distinguishes intrinsic motivation (doing something because it is inherently satisfying) from extrinsic motivation (doing it for an external reward). Intrinsic motivation is the durable kind, and it is fed by three needs: competence (I am getting better, I am skilled), autonomy (I have meaningful choices, I am the author of my actions), and relatedness (I connect with others). Design that satisfies these three produces players who play because they want to.

Extrinsic rewards, points, badges, loot, are powerful but treacherous. The overjustification effect warns that piling extrinsic rewards onto an already-intrinsically-fun activity can reduce the intrinsic enjoyment; players start grinding for the badge instead of savoring the play. Extrinsic rewards work best as a scaffold to draw players toward intrinsic fun, then fade, not as a permanent replacement for it.

Law.
Competence, autonomy, relatedness. If a feature does not feed at least one of these three intrinsic needs, ask whether it is engaging players or merely tricking them. Long-term retention comes from intrinsic satisfaction, not from rewards alone.

Reward schedules

How rewards are timed shapes behavior as much as what they are. Behavioral psychology's reinforcement schedules apply directly. Fixed schedules (a reward every Nth action or every N seconds) are predictable and produce a steady, modest pace, often with a dip right after the reward. Variable schedules (rewards on an unpredictable average) produce the highest, most persistent engagement, this is the variable-ratio schedule behind loot drops, critical hits, and chest rolls. The unpredictability is precisely what makes "one more try" so hard to resist.

Danger.
Variable-ratio rewards are the same mechanism that powers slot machines and they can cross into exploitation, especially with real-money loot boxes or predatory monetization. Use unpredictability to create delight and surprise, not to manufacture compulsion. Designing for engagement and designing for addiction are different goals; know which one you are doing.

Player types and motivation profiles

Players are not interchangeable. Bartle's classic taxonomy (born from MUDs) sorts multiplayer players into Achievers (seek mastery and completion), Explorers (seek discovery and understanding), Socializers (seek connection), and Killers (seek competition and impact on others). Most modern frameworks add nuance, but the lesson holds: a single mechanic appeals to different players for different reasons. Leaderboards thrill Achievers and Killers while leaving Socializers cold; a rich world delights Explorers while boring Killers. Designing for a mix means offering several motivational on-ramps to the same content.

Tip.
When a feature feels flat in testing, ask which intrinsic need it serves and for which player type. A reward that means nothing to an Explorer may be exactly what an Achiever was waiting for; the fix may be targeting, not redesign.
🎮 Design This: Motivation Audit

Goal: analyze and improve a feature through the lens of motivation.

  1. Pick one feature from a game you know (a daily login, a skill tree, a ranking).
  2. Tag which intrinsic needs it feeds: competence, autonomy, relatedness.
  3. Identify its reward schedule (fixed or variable) and predict player behavior.
  4. Flag any part that risks the overjustification effect or compulsion.
  5. Redesign it to add one autonomy-supporting choice for the player.

title: Genres as Design Lenses
html:
Genre is a toolbox, not a cage

A genre is a cluster of conventions: mechanics, structures, and player expectations that tend to travel together. Genres are usually defined by their core verbs and challenge type (a platformer is about precise jumping; a 4X is about explore-expand-exploit-exterminate). For designers, a genre is best used as a lens: a set of solved problems and proven patterns you can borrow, remix, or deliberately break. You do not have to obey a genre, but you should understand it before you defy it, because players arrive with its expectations pre-installed.

Conventions carry expectations

Every convention is a promise to the player. When you adopt a genre, you inherit its implicit contract: a roguelike player expects permadeath and run-based progression; a Metroidvania player expects a connected map gated by abilities; a survival player expects resource scarcity and a crafting tree. Meeting these expectations makes your game instantly legible, players already know the controls and goals. Breaking an expectation is a powerful design move, but only deliberately: a roguelike that secretly preserves progress can feel either brilliant or broken depending on whether the subversion is intentional and well-communicated.

genres as lenses (core verb + expectation)
What each lens foregrounds, and the contract it implies: PLATFORMER — verb: jump → precise movement, fair hazards, tight feel SHOOTER — verb: aim/fire → readable enemies, gunfeel, sightlines RPG — verb: grow → numbers go up, builds, meaningful choice STRATEGY — verb: plan → information, tradeoffs, long decision horizon ROGUELIKE — verb: risk → permadeath, run variety, meta-progression PUZZLE — verb: solve → one elegant solution, the aha moment SIM — verb: tend → systems to understand and optimize HORROR — verb: survive → vulnerability, scarcity, denied power

Mixing genres

Most fresh designs come from combining lenses. The deck-builder roguelike fused card games with run-based permadeath. The survival-crafting-sandbox fused three lenses into a dominant modern genre. The trick is to ask what each lens is for and whether the combination creates new interesting decisions. A genre mash-up works when the lenses reinforce each other (roguelike runs make a deck-builder's randomness feel fresh each time) and fails when they fight (a relaxing farming sim bolted to a twitch-reflex boss can whiplash the player out of both moods).

Borrowing a mechanic across genres

Lenses let you transplant a single mechanic into an unexpected home. Take the stamina bar from action games and drop it into a strategy game, and suddenly you have a tension and pacing tool you did not have before. Take fog of war from strategy and add it to a horror game, and you have manufactured dread. When borrowing, import the mechanic and its purpose. A mechanic that solved a specific problem in its home genre will only shine if your game has an equivalent problem for it to solve.

Tip.
Before inventing a mechanic from scratch, ask: which genre already solved a problem like mine? Borrowing a proven, expectation-laden pattern and re-skinning it for your context is faster and more reliable than designing in a vacuum.

Why expectation literacy matters

Knowing genres deeply makes you a better designer even when you ignore them, because it tells you exactly which expectations you are spending. Every deviation from genre convention is a withdrawal from the player's trust account; some are worth it (a signature twist that defines your game) and some just confuse (changing the jump button for no reason). Spend your deviations on the things that make your game special, and conform on everything else so the player's limited attention goes to what is genuinely new.

Law.
Conform where it does not matter, deviate where it does. Innovation budget is finite. Reuse genre conventions for the boring parts so the player's attention and patience are free for your one bold idea.
🎮 Design This: Genre Mash-Up

Goal: design a concept by fusing two genre lenses.

  1. Pick two genres from the lens table that are rarely combined.
  2. State the core verb and key expectation each lens brings.
  3. Describe the new interesting decision the fusion creates.
  4. Name one expectation you will deliberately break, and how you will signal it.
  5. Name two conventions you will keep unchanged to save innovation budget.

title: Prototyping and Playtesting
html:
Fail cheap, fail fast

The single most important habit in game design is testing ideas before you invest in them. A design only exists as a guess until a real person plays it. Prototyping is building the cheapest possible version that answers a specific question, and playtesting is putting it in front of players to get the answer. The discipline is to make the throwaway version, learn, and discard, before sinking weeks into art and code for a loop that was never fun.

Paper and greybox prototypes

The cheapest prototype is paper. Index cards, dice, a hand-drawn grid, and tokens can test the core of a surprising range of games, especially anything turn-based, economic, or rules-driven. Paper forces you to make the rules explicit and lets you change a number in seconds rather than recompiling. You play all the hidden systems by hand (you are the computer), which is slow but reveals exactly where the interesting decisions are, or are not.

For real-time and spatial feel, the next step is the greybox: a playable build with placeholder grey shapes and no art, audio, or story. The greybox isolates the question is the core loop fun? from every confounding variable. If a featureless grey cube jumping between grey blocks is already satisfying, you have a game; if it is not, no art will rescue it. Greyboxing keeps you honest because there is nothing pretty to hide behind.

prototype ladder
Climb only as high as the question requires; stop the moment you have your answer. PAPER — cards/dice/grid; tests rules, economy, turn structure GREYBOX — placeholder shapes; tests core loop, feel, spatial pacing VERTICAL SLICE — one polished segment; tests the real experience Each prototype should answer ONE question. Write the question down before you build.

Running a playtest

A playtest is a controlled observation, not a sales demo. Recruit testers who resemble your real audience, and ideally fresh ones each round, since a player can only experience your tutorial for the first time once. Set up, then get out of the way. The cardinal rule: do not help. The instant you explain something, you have contaminated the test, because the real player at home will have no designer whispering hints. Bite your tongue and watch them struggle; the struggle is the data.

Law.
Watch what players DO, not what they SAY. Players are unreliable narrators of their own experience. They will rationalize, flatter you, and misdiagnose their own frustration. Their behavior, where they got stuck, what they ignored, when they sighed, is the truth.

Reading behavior over words

When a player says "the jump felt fine" but you watched them miss the same gap eleven times, believe your eyes. Track behavioral signals: where do they hesitate? What do they never touch? Where do they die repeatedly? When do they lean in versus check their phone? When do they quit? A player suggesting a solution ("you should add a map") is usually wrong about the fix but right that a problem exists, your job is to find the real cause behind the symptom they noticed.

Asking good questions

Question design makes or breaks a test. Avoid leading questions ("Wasn't that boss fight exciting?") which fish for the answer you want. Avoid yes/no questions, which yield no insight. Ask open, non-judgmental, behavior-anchored questions, and ask them after observation, not instead of it.

better playtest questions
BAD: "Did you like the combat?" (yes/no, leading) BAD: "Wasn't the second level great?" (fishing for praise) GOOD: "Tell me what you were trying to do there." GOOD: "What were you expecting to happen when you pressed that?" GOOD: "Walk me through what you thought your goal was." GOOD: "When did you feel most stuck? Most powerful?" Then shut up and let the silence pull out a real answer.
Tip.
Keep a fixed quota of fresh testers in reserve. First-time reactions to your tutorial and onboarding are a non-renewable resource; spend them deliberately, a few at a time, across the project rather than all at once.
🎮 Design This: Run a Mini Playtest

Goal: test one design question with a paper or greybox prototype and a single player.

  1. Write the ONE question your prototype must answer.
  2. Build the cheapest version that tests it (cards, grid, or grey shapes).
  3. Recruit one fresh tester; explain controls only, then stay silent.
  4. Log behavior: hesitations, repeated failures, ignored features, quit point.
  5. Ask three open, behavior-anchored questions; record DO versus SAY mismatches.

title: Writing a Tight One-Page Design Doc
html:
The doc is a tool, not a monument

A game design document (GDD) communicates the vision of a game clearly enough that a team can build it and that you can keep yourself honest. The old image of a 200-page bible written before a line of code is mostly dead; modern teams favor living, lightweight docs that evolve with the project. The most valuable artifact for any small project or pitch is the one-page design doc: if you cannot express the heart of your game on a single page, you do not yet understand it well enough to build it.

The discipline of one page

The constraint is the point. A single page forces ruthless prioritization. It forces you to identify the one verb, the one hook, the one aesthetic that defines the game, and to cut everything that is merely nice. A bloated doc hides a fuzzy vision; a tight doc exposes whether you actually have one. The one-pager is also the document people will actually read, your teammates, collaborators, and future self will skim one page but never thirty.

one-page GDD skeleton
Fill every line in one or two sentences. If a line is hard to fill, the design has a hole. TITLE & LOGLINE — the game in one vivid sentence PILLARS — 3 design pillars that every decision must serve CORE LOOP — the moment-to-moment verbs the player repeats PLAYER FANTASY — who the player gets to BE / DO KEY MECHANICS — the 2-3 signature systems, no more AESTHETIC TARGET — the kinds of fun + look/tone in a phrase PROGRESSION — how the experience grows over a session and over time SCOPE / PLATFORM — length, audience, where it runs, what it is NOT UNIQUE HOOK — the one thing that makes this game worth making

Pillars: your decision filter

The most useful section is the design pillars: two to four short phrases that capture what the game is fundamentally about. Pillars are not features; they are values. "Every death teaches a lesson," "the world reacts to everything," "calm and contemplative" are pillars. Their job is to settle arguments: when the team debates a feature, you ask "does it serve a pillar?" If not, cut it, however clever it is. Pillars turn a thousand future micro-decisions into one consistent vision and prevent feature creep, the slow accretion of unrelated ideas that dilute a game into mush.

The logline and the hook

The logline compresses the whole game into one evocative sentence ("a hand-drawn run-based duel where every card you play also heals your enemy"). If you can nail this, you can pitch the game in an elevator and people remember it. The unique hook answers the brutal question every pitch faces: why does this game need to exist, and why would anyone choose it over the hundred similar games? A doc with no clear hook describes a competent clone; the hook is the reason to build it.

Law.
If it does not serve a pillar, cut it. The one-page doc's greatest power is as a filter that protects scope. A focused small game beats an unfocused ambitious one, especially for a solo developer or small team.

Keeping the doc alive

A design doc is a snapshot of current intent, not a contract carved in stone. As playtests teach you what the game actually is (Lesson 9), update the doc, do not defend it. The danger of a beautiful doc is attachment: teams sometimes ship the doc instead of the fun, refusing to abandon a planned feature that testing proved hollow. The doc serves the game; never the reverse. Date your versions, keep a short change log, and treat every revision as evidence you are learning rather than failing.

Tip.
Write the one-pager BEFORE you prototype, then revise it AFTER each playtest. The gap between what you intended and what the game became is the most honest measure of your progress, and usually where the real game has been hiding.
🎮 Design This: Write a One-Page GDD

Goal: capture a complete game vision on a single page using the skeleton.

  1. Write a one-sentence logline so vivid a stranger could repeat it.
  2. Define exactly three design pillars (values, not features).
  3. Fill every line of the skeleton in one or two sentences each.
  4. State explicitly what the game is NOT, to lock your scope.
  5. Re-read it and cut one feature that does not serve a pillar.
